description_long:
Gridmaple attacks the largest energy load in most commercial buildings — HVAC — with software alone. Its diagnosis: buildings mostly run on static schedules programmed years ago, so they heat empty floors, pre-cool on mild days, and fight themselves between zones. The pitch is optimization without retrofit, using the building management system already installed.

The mechanism is a closed loop. Gridmaple connects to BMS platforms over BACnet — no sensors to install — and machine-learning models forecast occupancy and weather for each zone, computing an optimized schedule every 15 minutes. Adjustments flow back to the BMS within guardrails the facilities team sets, and every change is logged and reversible, a design choice that acknowledges facilities managers do not hand strangers the thermostat lightly. Utility-rate awareness adds a second lever, shifting load away from peak pricing and demand charges.

The claimed results are 15 to 30 percent energy savings on HVAC, and the verification method matters: measured against utility meters, not modeled estimates. The same data feeds emissions disclosures and utility rebate programs, which turns a cost-savings tool into part of the compliance stack for portfolio owners.

The customers are owners and operators of office, retail, and healthcare portfolios, from a single 80,000-square-foot building to portfolios of 200 sites, with pricing per building per year sized by floor area — a structure that scales with the customer's estate rather than with usage metering.

The wedge is the no-hardware claim. Most competing paths to building efficiency involve sensor retrofits or equipment replacement, with capital budgets and installation schedules to match; software riding the existing BMS can deploy across a portfolio at a pace hardware never will. Founded in 2021 and headquartered in New York City by controls engineers and a former utility analyst — a plausibly load-bearing combination for a product that must speak BACnet and utility tariffs with equal fluency — the company stands at 52 people.
